running-python-code.label: Running Python Code
running-python-code.summary: Use the ZoeSkoul browser code editor, Run button, and output panel to execute Python code.

project.title: Run and check simple Python programs

sc_runner_parts.title: Main parts of the runner
sc_runner_parts.prompt: Which answer correctly describes how you run Python code in this lesson?
sc_runner_parts.hint: Think about the editor, the Run button, and where printed text appears.

help.concept: This question checks whether you know the basic browser workflow: write code in the code editor, click Run, and read results in the output panel.
help.hint_1: Look for the option that mentions typing code first, then running it, then reading printed output.
help.hint_2: The correct answer includes all three parts: code editor, Run button, and output panel.

options.a: Type code in the code editor, click Run, and read the result in the output panel.
options.b: Write code in a document and wait for it to run automatically.
options.c: Type code into the output panel and click Run there.
options.d: Click Run before writing code so Python can create the program for you.

sc_print_purpose.title: What print does
sc_print_purpose.prompt: What does `print("Hello")` do when you click Run?
sc_print_purpose.hint: Focus on what appears after Python executes the line.

help.concept: This question checks the role of `print(...)`: it displays a value in the output panel.
help.hint_1: The line does not create a comment or ask for input; it sends text outward.
help.hint_2: Think about the exact word inside the quotes and where that word is shown after running.

options.a: It shows the text Hello in the output panel.
options.b: It turns Hello into a comment.
options.c: It removes the word Hello from the program.
options.d: It asks the learner to type Hello.

mc_valid_print_lines.title: Valid print statements
mc_valid_print_lines.prompt: Choose all lines that are valid Python print statements.
mc_valid_print_lines.hint: Check for the print name, parentheses, and properly closed quotes.

help.concept: A valid beginner `print(...)` statement needs correct spelling, matching parentheses, and complete string quotes when printing text.
help.hint_1: Any option with a missing quote or missing parenthesis is not valid syntax.
help.hint_2: Look for lines where `print` is followed by parentheses and the text string starts and ends with quotes.

mc_comments_behavior.title: What comments do
mc_comments_behavior.prompt: Choose all correct statements about comments in beginner Python code.
mc_comments_behavior.hint: Think about lines that start with `#`.

help.concept: Comments are notes for humans. Python ignores them during execution, so they do not produce output by themselves.
help.hint_1: A comment can explain code, but it is not executed like a `print(...)` statement.
help.hint_2: Select the statements that match `#` lines being ignored by Python and useful for explanation.

options.b: Python ignores a comment when the code runs.
options.c: A comment always appears in the output panel.
options.d: A comment is required on every line of code.

dr_run_steps.title: Order the run process
dr_run_steps.prompt: Put these actions in the correct order for checking a Python program in ZoeSkoul.
dr_run_steps.hint: Start with writing code and end with reading the result.

help.concept: This question checks the normal workflow for using the browser runner: write code, run it, then inspect the output.
help.hint_1: You cannot inspect output before the program has been run.
help.hint_2: The correct sequence begins in the code editor, then uses Run, then finishes in the output panel.

tokens.t1: Type Python code in the code editor

dr_program_flow.title: Top-to-bottom execution
dr_program_flow.prompt: Arrange the printed lines in the order Python produces them for this code: `print("One")` then `print("Two")`.
dr_program_flow.hint: Arrange the pieces in the order Python uses them.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

fb_comment_symbol.title: Comment marker
fb_comment_symbol.prompt: Complete the Python comment line with the correct symbol.
fb_comment_symbol.hint: Look at the blank, then choose the word or symbol that makes the idea correct.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Pick the choice that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

fb_print_output_word.title: Predict simple output
fb_print_output_word.prompt: Fill in the missing output for this code.
fb_print_output_word.hint: Look at the exact text inside the quotes.

help.concept: When `print(...)` contains a string, Python displays that exact string in the output panel.
help.hint_1: The output matches the word between the quotation marks.
help.hint_2: Do not add extra punctuation or code words; use the printed text itself.

ci_print_greeting.title: Print a greeting
ci_print_greeting.prompt: Write Python code that prints exactly `Hello, ZoeSkoul!` on one line.
ci_print_greeting.hint: Use one `print(...)` statement with the exact text.

help.concept: This exercise practices the simplest run cycle: write one valid `print(...)` statement and check the exact output.
help.hint_1: Put the message inside quotes so Python treats it as text.
help.hint_2: Your program should produce one line of output with the greeting and punctuation exactly as shown.

ci_two_lines.title: Print two lines in order
ci_two_lines.prompt: Write Python code that prints `First` on one line and `Second` on the next line.

help.concept: This exercise checks top-to-bottom execution: each `print(...)` runs in order and creates its own output line.
help.hint_1: The first line of code should produce the first line of output.
help.hint_2: Write one print for `First` and another print below it for `Second`.

ci_comment_and_output.title: Use a comment and print
ci_comment_and_output.prompt: Write Python code with a comment on the first line and a `print(...)` statement on the second line that prints `Ready`.
ci_comment_and_output.hint: Read the coding task and identify what the program should print.

help.concept: This coding exercise checks whether the program produces the requested output.
help.hint_1: Start with the lesson example, then remove choices that do not match this task.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that directly matches the question without relying on answer wording.

ci_comment_and_output.starterCode:
# Add a comment here
# Then write a print statement below

ci_fix_quotes.title: Print quoted text correctly
ci_fix_quotes.prompt: Write Python code that prints exactly `Learning Python`.
ci_fix_quotes.hint: Read the task "Print quoted text correctly" and identify the required result.

help.concept: This coding exercise checks whether your code produces the requested result for: "Print quoted text correctly".
help.hint_1: Write the code that creates exactly the requested output.
help.hint_2: Click Run and compare the output panel with the expected result.

ci_print_expression.title: Print an expression result
ci_print_expression.prompt: Write Python code that prints the result of `3 + 4`.
ci_print_expression.hint: You can place a math expression directly inside `print(...)`.

help.concept: Python can evaluate a simple expression first and then print the resulting value.
help.hint_1: Do not put the expression in quotes if you want Python to calculate it.
help.hint_2: Use `print(...)` around the addition so the output panel shows the computed number.

ci_print_expression.starterCode: # Print the result of an addition

sketch_run_cycle.title: How code runs in ZoeSkoul
sketch_run_cycle.bodyMarkdown:
In this course, you write Python code in the **code editor**, click **Run**, and read the result in the **output panel**.

A Python program is a list of instructions. Python reads them from top to bottom. When it reaches a `print(...)` statement, it sends text or values to the output panel.

Example:

```python
print("Hello")
print("Python")
```

Walk through it:
- The first line prints `Hello`
- The second line prints `Python`
- The output panel shows the lines in the same order

So the output is:

```text
Hello
Python
```

sketch_print_and_strings.title: Using print with text
sketch_print_and_strings.bodyMarkdown:
The `print(...)` statement displays something in the output panel. Text in Python is usually written inside quotes.

Example:

```python
print("I am learning Python")
```

Here is what each part does:
- `print` tells Python to display something
- `(` and `)` wrap the value to display
- `"I am learning Python"` is a string value

You can also print numbers:

```python
print(7)
print(3 + 2)
```

This prints the value of each expression.

Step through it: read each statement from top to bottom. Notice which names are created, which values are passed into built-in tools, and what output the code is meant to show.

sketch_comments_and_errors.title: Comments and simple syntax mistakes
sketch_comments_and_errors.bodyMarkdown:
A comment starts with `#`. Python ignores comments when the code runs.

Example:

```python
# This line is a comment
print("Ready")
```

Only `Ready` appears in the output panel.

Beginners also see syntax mistakes sometimes. A syntax mistake means Python cannot understand the code as written.

For example, this is incorrect because the closing quote is missing:

```python
print("Hello)
```

This version is correct:

```python
print("Hello")
```

Step through it:
- The opening quote starts the string
- The closing quote ends the string
- The closing parenthesis finishes the `print(...)` statement
